content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Reasoning Prompts Dramatically Boost LLM Mathematical Problem Solving",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often hallucinate or make logical errors when solving multi-step mathematical problems, especially outside their training distribution. Existing prompting techniques, such as chain-of-thought or self-consistency, yield only incremental improvements.",
  "motivation": "Despite advances, LLMs still underperform humans in mathematical reasoning due to rigid reasoning paths and a lack of error correction. Improving robustness and accuracy is critical as LLMs are increasingly used in STEM education and research. This work aims to unlock fundamentally new capabilities in LLMs by rethinking prompt design.",
  "method": "We introduce Counterfactual Chain-of-Thought Prompting (CF-CoT), where the prompt explicitly requests the model to generate, for each reasoning step, both the 'most likely' next step and a plausible counterfactual alternative, then asks the model to compare these before proceeding. This dual-path exploration is recursively applied at each step, promoting deeper verification and error correction. The model is ultimately prompted to synthesize its reasoning, having considered multiple alternatives, before producing a final answer.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate CF-CoT on GSM8K, MATH, and MathQA, comparing against standard CoT, self-consistency, and least-to-most prompting. Accuracy, reasoning trace consistency, and error correction rate are primary metrics. Human expert evaluation will also be used to assess reasoning plausibility in challenging cases."
}
